@@ -36,7 +36,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-322g","title":"Create 'Starting a New Project' guide section","description":"# Task: Create 'Starting a New Project' Guide Section ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Daily Workflow & Getting Started Guide (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2cvc) ## Description The wizard uses "my-first-project" as an example, but doesn't explain the general pattern for starting real projects. Users need guidance on: - Creating project folders - Initializing git - Setting up with Claude ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/launch-onboarding/page.tsx or new /learn/new-project page \`\`\`tsx

Starting a New Project

Ready to build something? Here's the pattern:

1. Create a session for your project

This creates a persistent workspace named "my-awesome-app".

2. Create and navigate to a project folder

3. Start Claude and describe your project

Tell Claude what you want to build. For example:

"Create a React app with TypeScript that shows a todo list"
Claude will set up the project structure, install dependencies, and start building. You can guide it step by step or give it the whole vision at once.
\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Shows 3-step pattern (session → folder → Claude) - [ ] Uses concrete example project - [ ] Includes example prompt for Claude - [ ] Explains what Claude will do - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - Different color from daily workflow to distinguish - Example project should be relatable (todo app is classic) - Emphasize that Claude does the heavy lifting","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:00.569413-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:22:12.203033-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:22:12.203033-05:00","close_reason":"Already implemented in launch-onboarding with: 3-step pattern (ntm new → mkdir cd → cc), example project 'my-awesome-app', example Claude prompt for React todo app, GuideTip explaining Claude's capabilities, cyan color scheme distinct from daily workflow.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-322g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-s4cg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:33.220094-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-325","title":"Implement agent auth functional tests","description":"# Task: Implement agent auth functional tests ## Context Part of EPIC: Enhanced Doctor with Functional Tests (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-t9i) ## What to Do Create functional tests for AI agent authentication: ### check_claude_auth() 1. Check if claude binary exists (skip if not) 2. Check if ~/.claude/config.json exists 3. Try claude --print-system-info (low-cost API check) 4. Return: 0=OK, 1=fail, 2=skip ### check_codex_auth() 1. Check if codex binary exists 2. Check for OPENAI_API_KEY env var 3. Check for API key in ~/.zshrc.local 4. Return status ### check_gemini_auth() 1. Check if gemini binary exists 2. Check for GOOGLE_API_KEY or GEMINI_API_KEY 3. Check for credentials file 4. Return status ## Acceptance Criteria - Each check has clear pass/fail criteria - Skipped if tool not installed (status 2) - Auth issues detected reliably - No false positives ## Files to Modify - scripts/lib/doctor.sh: Add check functions","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:45:18.598224-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:57:34.018612-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:57:34.018612-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented check_claude_auth(), check_codex_auth(), check_gemini_auth() with config/API key checks","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-325","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-01s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:34.958137-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-32g","title":"[ubu.9] Handle LTS-to-LTS upgrade path special case","description":"# Handle LTS-to-LTS Upgrade Path Special Case ## Purpose Ubuntu LTS releases can upgrade directly to the next LTS (22.04 → 24.04) OR follow the regular path through intermediate releases. We need to handle this correctly. ## Background Ubuntu upgrade paths have special rules: - **Normal releases**: Must upgrade sequentially (24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10) - **LTS releases**: Can upgrade directly to next LTS (22.04 → 24.04) - **LTS to normal**: Requires enabling "Prompt=normal" in /etc/update-manager/release-upgrades ## Default Behavior By default, do-release-upgrade on LTS systems only shows LTS upgrades. To see non-LTS upgrades, need to modify configuration: ```bash # In /etc/update-manager/release-upgrades # Change: Prompt=lts # To: Prompt=normal ``` ## Implementation ### Detection ```bash # Check if current system is LTS ubuntu_is_lts() { local version=$(ubuntu_get_version_string) # LTS versions: 22.04, 24.04, 26.04, etc. [[ "$version" =~ ^[0-9]+\.04$ ]] } ``` ### Path Calculation for LTS Systems ```bash ubuntu_calculate_upgrade_path() { local target="$1" local current=$(ubuntu_get_version_string) if ubuntu_is_lts; then # Check if we should go LTS-to-LTS or through normal releases local next_lts=$(ubuntu_get_next_lts "$current") if ubuntu_version_gte "$next_lts" "$target"; then # Target is the next LTS or earlier - go direct echo "$next_lts" else # Target is beyond next LTS - need to go through normal releases # Enable normal release channel ubuntu_enable_normal_releases ubuntu_calculate_sequential_path "$current" "$target" fi else ubuntu_calculate_sequential_path "$current" "$target" fi } ``` ### Enable Normal Releases ```bash ubuntu_enable_normal_releases() { local config="/etc/update-manager/release-upgrades" if [[ -f "$config" ]]; then # Backup original cp "$config" "${config}.acfs-backup" # Change Prompt=lts to Prompt=normal sed -i "s/^Prompt=lts$/Prompt=normal/" "$config" log_info "Enabled normal release upgrades (was LTS-only)" fi } ``` ## Example Upgrade Paths ### From 22.04 LTS to 25.10 Option A (LTS path first): - 22.04 → 24.04 (LTS hop) - 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 (normal path) Option B (all normal): - 22.04 → 22.10 → 23.04 → 23.10 → 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 **We choose Option A** - fewer hops, more stable. ### From 24.04 LTS to 25.10 - 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 (normal path, 3 hops) ## Configuration Restoration After reaching target version, restore original LTS-only setting: ```bash ubuntu_restore_lts_only() { local config="/etc/update-manager/release-upgrades" local backup="${config}.acfs-backup" if [[ -f "$backup" ]]; then mv "$backup" "$config" log_info "Restored LTS-only release setting" fi } ``` ## Testing Notes - Test from 22.04: Verify LTS-to-LTS hop works - Test from 24.04: Verify normal path enables correctly - Test config restoration after completion","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:24:05.237039-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:39:24.893274-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:39:24.893274-05:00","close_reason":"Added LTS detection (ubuntu_is_lts - even years only), next LTS calculation (ubuntu_get_next_lts), and release channel management (ubuntu_enable_normal_releases, ubuntu_restore_lts_only).","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-32g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:24:40.623353-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-367d","title":"Audit and expand Jargon component terms","description":"# Task: Audit and Expand Jargon Component Terms ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Jargon System & Documentation Polish (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-7i45) ## Description The Jargon component provides tooltip definitions for technical terms, but coverage is incomplete and some definitions are too technical. Need a comprehensive audit. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/components/jargon.tsx ### Terms to audit/add: - VPS - Virtual Private Server (already exists, verify quality) - SSH - Secure Shell (verify quality) - Terminal - text interface (verify quality) - Command line - same as terminal (alias?) - Root - superuser/admin account - sudo - run as superuser - Ubuntu user - regular user account - tmux - terminal multiplexer - Session - persistent terminal environment - Repository/Repo - code storage folder - Git - version control system - API key - authentication token - Environment variable - system setting - Port - network endpoint - IP address - network address - zsh - shell program - Bash - shell program ### Quality criteria for definitions: - No jargon in the definition itself - One sentence maximum for tooltip - Relatable analogy if possible - Consistent tone ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All terms listed above have definitions - [ ] Definitions are beginner-friendly (no technical jargon) - [ ] Tooltip text fits comfortably (not too long) - [ ] All wizard pages use Jargon wrappers appropriately - [ ] Mobile tooltip experience is good (tap to show) ## UI/UX Notes - Tooltips should be quick reads (2-3 seconds) - Consider linking to glossary for longer explanations - Test on mobile — touch targets and positioning","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:46.425151-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:46.425151-05:00"} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-367d","title":"Audit and expand Jargon component terms","description":"# Task: Audit and Expand Jargon Component Terms ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Jargon System & Documentation Polish (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-7i45) ## Description The Jargon component provides tooltip definitions for technical terms, but coverage is incomplete and some definitions are too technical. Need a comprehensive audit. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/components/jargon.tsx ### Terms to audit/add: - VPS - Virtual Private Server (already exists, verify quality) - SSH - Secure Shell (verify quality) - Terminal - text interface (verify quality) - Command line - same as terminal (alias?) - Root - superuser/admin account - sudo - run as superuser - Ubuntu user - regular user account - tmux - terminal multiplexer - Session - persistent terminal environment - Repository/Repo - code storage folder - Git - version control system - API key - authentication token - Environment variable - system setting - Port - network endpoint - IP address - network address - zsh - shell program - Bash - shell program ### Quality criteria for definitions: - No jargon in the definition itself - One sentence maximum for tooltip - Relatable analogy if possible - Consistent tone ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All terms listed above have definitions - [ ] Definitions are beginner-friendly (no technical jargon) - [ ] Tooltip text fits comfortably (not too long) - [ ] All wizard pages use Jargon wrappers appropriately - [ ] Mobile tooltip experience is good (tap to show) ## UI/UX Notes - Tooltips should be quick reads (2-3 seconds) - Consider linking to glossary for longer explanations - Test on mobile — touch targets and positioning","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:44:46.425151-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:30:20.58081-05:00"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-37sa","title":"Add p10k wizard handling instructions","description":"# Task: Add Powerlevel10k Wizard Handling ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Post-Install Transition Experience (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-r2np) ## Description On first zsh login, Powerlevel10k may launch a configuration wizard. This is unexpected and confusing for beginners. They don't know what it's asking or if they should configure it. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/reconnect-ubuntu/page.tsx Add a GuideTip or AlertCard: \`\`\`tsx Prompt customization wizard? When you first connect, you might see a "Powerlevel10k configuration wizard" asking about fonts and prompt style. You can:
  • Press q to quit and use defaults (recommended for now)
  • Or go through it if you want to customize your prompt appearance

You can always run p10k configure later to customize.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Warns about possible p10k wizard - [ ] Clear instruction to press 'q' to skip - [ ] Mentions ability to configure later - [ ] Non-scary tone (it's a feature, not a problem) ## UI/UX Notes - Use GuideTip (not warning) — this is informational - Keep it brief; most users will just press q - "Recommended for now" guides beginners to the simpler path","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:43:03.190685-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:43:03.190685-05:00"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3ci","title":"Fix acfs doctor --json output (pure JSON + correct counts)","description":"scripts/lib/doctor.sh --json currently prints section headings before JSON, doesn’t increment pass/warn/fail counters in JSON mode, and doesn’t escape strings. Make --json emit only valid JSON on stdout with correct summary counts (and handle missing /etc/os-release).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2025-12-20T13:44:19.646755-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T13:49:30.854595-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T13:49:30.854595-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed scripts/lib/doctor.sh --json to emit pure valid JSON (no headings), increment counters in JSON mode, and JSON-escape strings; also handles missing /etc/os-release."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3edj","title":"[EPIC] Authentication Flow for Headless VPS","description":"# Epic: Authentication Flow for Headless VPS ## Problem Statement When users run auth commands like `claude` or `gh auth login` on their VPS, these tools try to open a browser. But the VPS has no browser! The tool outputs a URL that the user must manually open on their laptop. This flow is: 1. Not explained anywhere in the wizard 2. Confusing when it says "Opening browser..." but nothing happens 3. Even more confusing if xdg-open is installed and fails silently Additionally, the accounts page shows too many services at once, and subscription requirements (Claude Pro, ChatGPT Pro) aren't prominent enough. ## Background & Reasoning OAuth flows in CLI tools assume a local machine with a browser. Headless servers require the "device flow" where a URL is displayed for manual opening. Users need to: 1. See the URL in the terminal 2. Copy it 3. Paste into their laptop's browser 4. Complete the OAuth flow 5. Return to terminal This is standard for developers but completely foreign to beginners. Without guidance, they'll think the auth failed. ## User Story As a beginner authenticating services on my VPS, I want to understand the device authentication flow, So that I can successfully connect my accounts without confusion. ## Success Criteria 1. User understands that VPS has no browser 2. User knows to copy the URL and paste in laptop browser 3. User knows to return to terminal after browser auth completes 4. User understands which services need paid subscriptions 5. User feels safe skipping non-essential services ## Scope - Add "Headless Authentication" explanation to status-check page - Enhance accounts page to de-emphasize optional services - Add subscription requirement warnings prominently - Add "What happens if I skip?" reassurance - Consider reordering: Essential only by default, expand for more ## Dependencies - Epic D: First SSH Connection (user must be connected to VPS) ## UI/UX Requirements - Maintain current polish for desktop and mobile - Use AlertCard for the headless auth explanation - Subscription warnings should use distinct visual treatment - "Skip" button needs reassurance text - Mobile: auth command copy must be easy ## Technical Approach 1. Add prominent AlertCard to status-check page explaining device flow 2. Add "Copy URL, paste in laptop browser" instructions 3. Enhance service cards with subscription badges 4. Default-collapse Recommended and Optional tiers 5. Add reassurance text near skip button ## Estimated Effort Medium (2 wizard page updates) ## Priority Justification P1 (High) - Without this, users cannot authenticate and cannot use the tools they just installed.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:35:19.713077-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:35:19.713077-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3edj","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-q7r3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:37:54.358146-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} @@ -160,7 +160,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-gl9","title":"ShellCheck: fix SC2235 in services-setup guard prompt","description":"ShellCheck flags a subshell grouping in around the Claude Git Safety Guard prompt condition (SC2235). Replace the (..) grouping with a { ..; } block to avoid subshell and satisfy ShellCheck.","acceptance_criteria":"- In scripts/services-setup.sh line 422: if [[ "$SERVICES_SETUP_NONINTERACTIVE" != "true" ]] && ([[ -t 0 ]] || [[ -r /dev/tty ]]); then ^-- SC2235 (style): Use { ..; } instead of (..) to avoid subshell overhead. For more information: https://www.shellcheck.net/wiki/SC2235 -- Use { ..; } instead of (..) to av... passes with no SC2235","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:14:01.146149-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T18:14:41.801909-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T18:14:41.801909-05:00","close_reason":"Replaced subshell grouping with { ..; } in services-setup guard prompt condition; shellcheck passes."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-glt2","title":"Add key auth troubleshooting (fallback to password)","description":"# Task: Add Key Auth Troubleshooting ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Post-Install Transition Experience ## Problem If SSH key authentication fails when reconnecting as ubuntu, users are stuck. They need: 1. Understanding of why it might fail 2. Fallback to password authentication 3. How to fix the key setup ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/reconnect-ubuntu/page.tsx Add troubleshooting section: \`\`\`tsx
Key authentication not working?

Try with password first

Remove the -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 part and use your root password:

Then fix the key

Once connected, run the installer again to set up the key properly:

If password authentication also fails, check that your VPS is running in your provider's control panel.
\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Clear fallback path (remove -i flag, use password) - [ ] Instructions to fix key setup (re-run installer) - [ ] Collapsible to not clutter happy path - [ ] Final fallback (check VPS is running) ## Why This Matters Users who get stuck at reconnection have completed 90% of setup. A clear recovery path saves them from starting over.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:55:58.191747-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:03:05.319564-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:03:05.319564-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: ssh-connect/page.tsx has extensive TROUBLESHOOTING section including 'Permission denied' with password solutions"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-grfs","title":"Add 'What is a Terminal?' explanation section","description":"# Task: Add 'What is a Terminal?' Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Terminal Onboarding Foundation (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ux1f) ## Description Add a beginner-friendly explanation of what a terminal is BEFORE asking users to install one. Currently we say "install a terminal" without explaining what it is or why they need it. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/install-terminal/page.tsx Add GuideExplain component at the top of SimplerGuide section: \`\`\`tsx A terminal is a text-based interface for controlling your computer. Instead of clicking buttons, you type commands. It might look intimidating at first, but you'll quickly get comfortable with the few commands you need.

Think of it like texting your computer: you write a short message (command), press Enter, and the computer responds. \`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explanation appears before install instructions - [ ] Uses SimplerGuide/GuideExplain component for consistency - [ ] Tone is friendly and reassuring, not technical - [ ] Works well on mobile (no text overflow) ## UI/UX Notes - Keep it brief - users are eager to move forward - Use analogy (texting computer) to make it relatable - Avoid jargon like "command line interface" or "shell"","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:39:21.243007-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:00.450157-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:00.450157-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: install-terminal/page.tsx has GuideExplain term='Terminal' with full explanation"} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","title":"Clarify VPS provider account vs VPS instance distinction","description":"# Task: Clarify Account vs Instance Distinction ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem The wizard has "Rent VPS" and "Create VPS" as separate steps. Beginners don't understand: - "Rent VPS" = Create an account with a provider (like signing up for Netflix) - "Create VPS" = Launch an actual server (like picking a show to watch) This causes confusion when they complete step 4 and think they have a VPS already. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx (end of page) and create-vps/page.tsx (top) Add transition text at end of rent-vps: \`\`\`tsx Account created? Great! Now you need to actually launch a server. Having an account is like having a Netflix subscription — you still need to pick something to watch. The next step creates your actual VPS. \`\`\` Add clarification at top of create-vps: \`\`\`tsx

You have an account with your VPS provider. Now let's create the actual server (the VPS instance) that will run your development environment.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Clear distinction between account and instance - [ ] Relatable analogy (Netflix subscription vs picking a show) - [ ] Transition text links the two steps - [ ] Users understand they have two passwords (account + root) ## Why This Matters This confusion directly causes the "wrong password" issue at SSH connection. Users try their Hetzner account password instead of their VPS root password.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:54:03.255992-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:28:44.511566-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a9nk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:17.500583-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","title":"Clarify VPS provider account vs VPS instance distinction","description":"# Task: Clarify Account vs Instance Distinction ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem The wizard has "Rent VPS" and "Create VPS" as separate steps. Beginners don't understand: - "Rent VPS" = Create an account with a provider (like signing up for Netflix) - "Create VPS" = Launch an actual server (like picking a show to watch) This causes confusion when they complete step 4 and think they have a VPS already. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx (end of page) and create-vps/page.tsx (top) Add transition text at end of rent-vps: \`\`\`tsx Account created? Great! Now you need to actually launch a server. Having an account is like having a Netflix subscription — you still need to pick something to watch. The next step creates your actual VPS. \`\`\` Add clarification at top of create-vps: \`\`\`tsx

You have an account with your VPS provider. Now let's create the actual server (the VPS instance) that will run your development environment.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Clear distinction between account and instance - [ ] Relatable analogy (Netflix subscription vs picking a show) - [ ] Transition text links the two steps - [ ] Users understand they have two passwords (account + root) ## Why This Matters This confusion directly causes the "wrong password" issue at SSH connection. Users try their Hetzner account password instead of their VPS root password.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:54:03.255992-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:30:15.013186-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:30:15.013186-05:00","close_reason":"Added: (1) Transition AlertCard at end of rent-vps with Netflix analogy explaining account vs instance, (2) Clarifying text at top of create-vps page","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a9nk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:17.500583-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hao","title":"[codex.3] Document OpenAI account types distinction","description":"## Goal Add clear documentation explaining the difference between OpenAI API accounts and ChatGPT accounts. ## Why This Matters Many users are confused because OpenAI has TWO distinct account/auth systems: ### 1. OpenAI API Account - **Purpose**: Programmatic API access (for developers building apps) - **Auth**: OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable - **Billing**: Pay-per-token, separate API billing - **Where**: platform.openai.com - **What uses it**: OpenAI SDK, custom applications - **NOT used by**: codex-cli ### 2. ChatGPT Account (Pro/Plus/Teams) - **Purpose**: Consumer chat interface and coding tools - **Auth**: OAuth via web browser (email/password or SSO) - **Billing**: Monthly subscription - **Where**: chat.openai.com - **What uses it**: ChatGPT website, codex-cli, mobile app - **Used by**: codex-cli! ## Documentation to Create/Update 1. Add section to README.md or create docs/agent-auth.md 2. Update acfs/onboard/lessons/04_agents_login.md with clarification 3. Consider adding to wizard website (apps/web/) ## Key Points to Document 1. codex-cli uses ChatGPT account, NOT API key 2. No OPENAI_API_KEY needed for codex-cli 3. Authentication flow: run "codex auth" → browser opens → login → done 4. May need to enable device access in ChatGPT settings 5. caam can backup/restore codex credentials just like claude/gemini ## Acceptance Criteria 1. Clear explanation of the two account types 2. Explicit statement that codex-cli uses ChatGPT, not API 3. Step-by-step auth guide for codex-cli 4. Reference to ChatGPT security settings if needed","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:37.395114-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:45:34.456497-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:45:34.456497-05:00","close_reason":"Updated acfs/onboard/lessons/04_agents_login.md with clear table distinguishing ChatGPT (OAuth) vs API (key) account types. docs/codex-auth-research.md already has technical details.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hao","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ua5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:36.053709-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hby","title":"Implement AI agent updates (claude, codex, gemini)","description":"## What Implement the 'agents' category for acfs-update: - Claude Code: claude update (built-in) or reinstall via official installer - Codex CLI: bun update -g @openai/codex - Gemini CLI: bun update -g @google/gemini-cli ## Technical Details - Claude Code has native update: ~/.local/bin/claude update - If claude update fails, fall back to reinstall via installer - Codex/Gemini are bun global packages - simple bun update ## Considerations - Claude update may require re-authentication after major updates -
